expanded_description:
Economic growth refers to the increase in the quantity and quality of goods and services produced by an economy over time. More precisely, it is typically measured as the annual percentage change in real GDP per capita (inflation-adjusted output per person). Economic growth is the most important determinant of living standards over the long run. A country that grows at 2% per year will double its income per capita every 35 years; a country that grows at 4% per year will double it every 18 years. Small differences in growth rates, compounded over decades, lead to vast differences in living standards.

Understanding economic growth is essential for analyzing why some countries are rich and others are poor, for evaluating the impact of policies on long-run prosperity, and for assessing the sustainability of growth. Economic growth is not automatic; it depends on factors such as capital accumulation, labor force growth, technological progress, institutional quality, and political stability. The study of economic growth is central to macroeconomics and development economics.

In this section, we will define economic growth, distinguish between real GDP and real GDP per capita, analyze the power of compounding (the rule of 70), discuss the difference between growth in potential output and actual output (business cycles), and examine the importance of long-run growth for living standards. You will learn to calculate growth rates, use the rule of 70 to estimate doubling times, and understand why even small differences in growth rates matter enormously over time.

core_concept.principle:
Economic growth is the increase in the quantity and quality of goods and services produced by an economy over time. The most common measure is the annual percentage change in real GDP per capita (real GDP divided by population).

Key Measures:

• Real GDP: The total value of all final goods and services produced, adjusted for inflation. Measures the size of the economy.
• Real GDP per capita: Real GDP divided by population. Measures average output per person (a proxy for average living standards).
• Growth rate: $g = \frac{Y_t - Y_{t-1}}{Y_{t-1}} \times 100\%$

Nominal vs. Real GDP:

• Nominal GDP: Valued at current prices. Changes due to both quantity and price changes.
• Real GDP: Valued at constant base-year prices. Changes due only to quantity changes (inflation adjusted).

The Rule of 70 (Doubling Time):

$\text{Doubling time (years)} = \frac{70}{\text{Annual growth rate (\%)}}$

Example: At 2% growth, doubling time = 70/2 = 35 years. At 4% growth, doubling time = 70/4 = 17.5 years.

Growth in Potential vs. Actual Output:

• Potential output (Y*): The maximum sustainable output the economy can produce given its resources (capital, labor, technology).
• Actual output (Y): The actual output produced, which fluctuates around potential due to business cycles.
• Output gap: $Y - Y^*$ (positive = overheating, negative = recession).
• Long-run growth: Increases in potential output (driven by capital accumulation, labor force growth, productivity).

Why Economic Growth Matters:

• Higher living standards: More goods and services per person (food, housing, healthcare, education, entertainment).
• Poverty reduction: Growth lifts people out of poverty (China's growth lifted 800 million out of poverty).
• Better health and education: Richer countries can afford better healthcare, education, and infrastructure.
• Political stability: Growth reduces social unrest and supports democratic institutions.
• Environmental concerns: Growth can be sustainable with green technology.

Historical Perspective:

• Pre-industrial revolution: Growth was near zero (living standards stagnant for centuries).
• Industrial revolution (1760-1840): Growth accelerated to 0.5-1.0% per year.
• 20th century: Growth averaged 2-3% per year in developed countries.
• East Asian miracle (1960-2000): Growth of 6-10% per year (Japan, South Korea, Taiwan, China).
• Sub-Saharan Africa: Slower growth (1-2% per year).

Canada's Growth:

• Canada's real GDP per capita has grown at about 1.5-2.0% per year since 1980.
• Canada's growth has been driven by capital accumulation, labor force growth (immigration), and productivity improvements.
• Canada's growth has been slower than the US (2.0-2.5%) but faster than many European countries.
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine two countries: Country A grows at 2% per year, Country B grows at 4% per year. Initially, both have the same GDP per capita of \$20,000. After 35 years, Country A's GDP per capita doubles to \$40,000. After 35 years, Country B's GDP per capita doubles twice (since doubling time is 17.5 years) to \$80,000. After 70 years, Country A has \$80,000, Country B has \$320,000. The gap is enormous. This is the power of compounding.

Economic growth is not about short-run fluctuations (recessions and booms). Those are business cycles. Economic growth is about the long-run trend in potential output. A recession might cause output to fall temporarily, but growth resumes as the economy recovers.

Why do some countries grow faster than others? Economists point to factors such as:
• Investment in physical capital (machines, factories, infrastructure)
• Investment in human capital (education, skills, health)
• Technological progress (innovation, adoption of new technologies)
• Institutions (property rights, rule of law, honest government)
• Trade openness (access to global markets)
• Political stability and peace

Canada has grown steadily due to sound institutions, investment in education and infrastructure, and openness to trade (especially with the US). However, Canada's productivity growth has lagged the US in recent decades, a concern for policymakers.

The Bank of Canada and the federal government monitor growth closely. The Bank's monetary policy aims to smooth business cycles but cannot affect long-run growth. Long-run growth depends on structural policies: education, infrastructure, R&D, tax reform, and trade agreements.

image.alt: Graph showing real GDP per capita over time (1900-2024) for Canada, US, UK, and China; logarithmic scale shows exponential growth.
image.caption: Figure 13.1.1: Economic growth in real GDP per capita over the past century. Developed countries have grown steadily; developing countries like China have grown rapidly in recent decades.

sections.introduction:
Economic growth refers to the increase in the quantity and quality of goods and services produced by an economy over time. More precisely, it is typically measured as the annual percentage change in real GDP per capita (inflation-adjusted output per person). Economic growth is the most important determinant of living standards over the long run. A country that grows at 2% per year will double its income per capita every 35 years; a country that grows at 4% per year will double it every 18 years. Small differences in growth rates, compounded over decades, lead to vast differences in living standards.

Understanding economic growth is essential for analyzing why some countries are rich and others are poor, for evaluating the impact of policies on long-run prosperity, and for assessing the sustainability of growth. Economic growth is not automatic; it depends on factors such as capital accumulation, labor force growth, technological progress, institutional quality, and political stability. The study of economic growth is central to macroeconomics and development economics.

In this section, we will define economic growth, distinguish between real GDP and real GDP per capita, analyze the power of compounding (the rule of 70), discuss the difference between growth in potential output and actual output (business cycles), and examine the importance of long-run growth for living standards.

introduction_image.alt: World map showing GDP per capita growth rates by country (1960-2024); East Asia (China, South Korea) shows fastest growth; Sub-Saharan Africa shows slowest growth.
introduction_image.caption: Figure 13.1.2: Economic growth rates vary widely across countries. East Asian countries have experienced growth miracles; many Sub-Saharan African countries have grown slowly.

key_concept.definition:
Economic Growth: The increase in the quantity and quality of goods and services produced by an economy over time. Typically measured as the annual percentage change in real GDP per capita.

Real GDP (Gross Domestic Product):

• Total value of all final goods and services produced within a country, adjusted for inflation.
• Measured using constant base-year prices (e.g., 2017 dollars).
• Removes the effect of price changes, so only quantity changes matter.

Real GDP per Capita:

• Real GDP divided by population.
• Measures average output per person (a proxy for average living standards).
• Better measure of prosperity than total GDP (which can be large due to large population).

Growth Rate Calculation:

$g_t = \frac{Y_t - Y_{t-1}}{Y_{t-1}} \times 100\%$

where $Y_t$ is real GDP per capita in year t.

Rule of 70 (Doubling Time):

$\text{Doubling time (years)} = \frac{70}{\text{Annual growth rate (\%)}}$

• Derived from compound interest formula.
• At 2% growth: doubles every 35 years.
• At 4% growth: doubles every 17.5 years.
• At 7% growth: doubles every 10 years.

Potential vs. Actual Output:

• Potential output (Y*): Maximum sustainable output given resources (capital, labor, technology).
• Actual output (Y): Actual output, fluctuates around potential due to business cycles.
• Output gap: $Y - Y^*$ (positive = overheating, negative = recession).
• Long-run growth: Increases in potential output.
• Short-run fluctuations: Business cycles around potential.

Why Economic Growth Matters:

• Higher living standards (more goods and services per person).
• Poverty reduction (growth lifts people out of poverty).
• Better health and education (richer countries can afford more).
• Political stability (growth reduces social unrest).
• Environmental protection (richer countries can afford cleaner technology).

Historical Growth Rates:

• Pre-1700: Near 0% per year (living standards stagnant).
• 1700-1820: 0.1-0.3% per year (slow acceleration).
• 1820-1900: 0.5-1.0% per year (Industrial Revolution).
• 1900-1950: 1.0-1.5% per year.
• 1950-2024: 2.0-3.0% per year in developed countries.
• East Asian miracles: 6-10% per year (Japan, South Korea, Taiwan, China).
key_concept.context: The study of economic growth was revolutionized by Robert Solow (1956) with the Solow growth model, which explained growth through capital accumulation and technological progress. Before Solow, economists had little formal theory of growth. The 'growth miracles' of East Asia (Japan, South Korea, Taiwan, China) and the 'growth disasters' of Sub-Saharan Africa have motivated extensive research on the determinants of growth. The World Bank and IMF publish growth statistics; the Penn World Table provides cross-country growth data. Canada's growth has been steady but not spectacular, averaging about 1.5-2.0% per capita growth since 1980.

image.alt: Graph: World GDP per capita (log scale) from 1000 AD to 2024; flat until 1700, then exponential increase after Industrial Revolution.
image.caption: Figure 13.1.3: Economic growth was near zero for most of human history. The Industrial Revolution triggered sustained growth, and growth rates have accelerated over time.

classifications.type: Real GDP vs. Real GDP per Capita
classifications.value: Total real GDP measures the size of the economy; real GDP per capita measures average living standards
characteristics: Real GDP: Total output, adjusted for inflation. Useful for measuring economic power.
Real GDP per capita: Real GDP divided by population. Useful for comparing living standards.
China has higher total real GDP than the US (PPP-adjusted) but lower per capita GDP.
Canada's real GDP per capita is about \$55,000 (PPP-adjusted).
Growth in real GDP per capita = growth in real GDP - growth in population.
If population grows faster than output, per capita GDP falls (even if total GDP rises).
classifications.behavior:
Total real GDP is important for measuring the size of an economy and its global influence. China has surpassed the US in total real GDP (PPP-adjusted), but its per capita GDP is still much lower (about \$20,000 vs. \$70,000 in the US). This is because China's population is four times larger. Per capita GDP is a better measure of living standards.

For Canada, total real GDP is about \$2.2 trillion, and per capita GDP is about \$55,000 (PPP-adjusted). Canada's population growth (about 1% per year) means that real GDP must grow at least 1% just to keep per capita GDP constant. If real GDP grows at 2% and population grows at 1%, per capita GDP grows at 1%.

The formula: $g_{per\ capita} = g_{total} - n$, where $n$ is population growth rate. Canada's population growth (driven by immigration) is higher than many developed countries, so Canada needs faster total GDP growth to achieve the same per capita growth as countries with slower population growth.

image.alt: Bar chart: Real GDP and real GDP per capita for selected countries (US, China, India, Canada, Japan, Germany).
image.caption: Figure 13.1.4: Total real GDP vs. real GDP per capita. China has higher total GDP than the US (PPP-adjusted) but much lower per capita GDP.

examples.process: Canada: Total vs. Per Capita Growth
examples.explanation: From 2010 to 2024, Canada's total real GDP grew at about 2.0% per year. Population grew at about 1.0% per year. Real GDP per capita grew at about 1.0% per year. This is typical for developed countries.

examples.process: China: Rapid Total Growth, Slower Per Capita Growth
examples.explanation: China's total real GDP grew at 8-10% per year from 1980 to 2010, but population grew at 1-2% per year. Per capita growth was still very high (6-8%). As population growth has slowed (now near zero), per capita growth has approached total growth.

classifications.value: Small differences in growth rates lead to large differences in living standards over time due to compounding
characteristics: Compounding means that even small differences in growth rates become huge over decades.
classifications.behavior:
The rule of 70 is a convenient approximation for the doubling time of a quantity growing at a constant rate. It is derived from the compound interest formula: $Y_t = Y_0 e^{gt}$. Setting $Y_t = 2Y_0$ gives $t = \ln(2)/g \approx 0.693/g$. Multiplying by 100 to convert g from decimal to percentage gives $t \approx 70/g$ (since 0.693 × 100 = 69.3, rounded to 70).

The power of compounding is immense. A country that grows at 2% per year will take 35 years to double its income. A country that grows at 4% will take only 17.5 years. After 70 years, the 2% country will have doubled twice (4×), while the 4% country will have doubled four times (16×). The gap is enormous.

This explains why East Asian countries (which grew at 6-10% for decades) caught up so quickly. South Korea's per capita income was comparable to Ghana's in 1960; today, South Korea's income is 10 times higher. The difference is entirely due to growth rates (South Korea grew rapidly, Ghana grew slowly).

For Canada, a 1 percentage point difference in growth rate (e.g., 1.5% vs. 2.5%) makes a huge difference over a lifetime. A Canadian child born today would have income about 50% higher at retirement if growth is 2.5% instead of 1.5%.

image.alt: Graph: GDP per capita over time for countries growing at 1%, 2%, 3%, 4%, 5%; widening gap over time.
image.caption: Figure 13.1.5: The rule of 70 shows how small differences in growth rates compound into large differences in living standards over time.

examples.explanation: Since 1980, Canada's per capita growth has averaged about 1.5% per year, while US per capita growth has averaged about 2.0% per year. After 44 years, the US income per capita is about 1.25 times Canada's (2% vs. 1.5% compounding). This gap has widened over time.

China's per capita growth averaged about 8% per year from 1980 to 2020. Doubling time = 70/8 = 8.75 years. China doubled its per capita income every 8-9 years. Over 40 years, income increased by a factor of 2^(40/8.75) = 2^4.57 = about 24 times. Millions were lifted out of poverty.

classifications.value: Long-run growth is about increases in potential output; business cycles are short-run fluctuations around potential
characteristics: Potential output (Y*): Maximum sustainable output given resources (capital, labor, technology).
Actual output (Y): Actual output, fluctuates due to demand shocks.
Output gap = Y - Y* (positive = overheating, negative = recession).
Long-run growth: Increases in Y* (driven by supply-side factors).
Short-run fluctuations: Business cycles (driven by demand).
Monetary and fiscal policy can affect actual output in the short run but not potential output in the long run.
classifications.behavior:
It is important to distinguish between long-run economic growth (increases in potential output) and short-run business cycles (fluctuations in actual output around potential). Potential output is determined by the economy's productive capacity: capital stock, labor force, technology, and institutions. Actual output is determined by aggregate demand in the short run.

During a recession, actual output falls below potential (negative output gap). This does not mean the economy's growth potential has changed; it means the economy is operating below capacity. As the economy recovers, actual output returns to potential.

Long-run growth is about increasing potential output. This requires investment, innovation, education, and good institutions. Monetary policy cannot increase potential output; it can only smooth business cycles. Fiscal policy can affect potential output through public investment (infrastructure, education, R&D).

Canada's potential output growth has slowed from about 3% in the 1990s to about 1.5-2.0% today, due to slower productivity growth and slower labor force growth (aging population, though immigration has offset some of the decline). This is a concern for policymakers.

image.alt: Graph: Potential output (smooth upward trend) and actual output (fluctuating around trend); recessions shown as gaps.
image.caption: Figure 13.1.6: Potential output (Y*) grows steadily over time. Actual output (Y) fluctuates around potential due to business cycles. Long-run growth is about increasing Y*.

examples.explanation: During the 2008-2009 recession, actual output fell below potential (negative output gap). Potential output continued to grow, but actual output fell. The recovery closed the gap. Long-run growth (potential) was not affected (though some economists argue hysteresis can reduce potential).

During the 2020 pandemic, actual output fell sharply below potential. Potential output continued to grow slowly. The rapid recovery (2021-2022) closed the gap. The pandemic may have reduced potential output due to supply chain disruptions and labor force exits (hysteresis).

principles.principle: Small Differences in Growth Rates Compound into Large Differences in Living Standards Over Time
principles.statement: The most important fact about economic growth is that even small differences in growth rates, sustained over decades, lead to enormous differences in living standards. A country that grows at 2% per year will double its income every 35 years; a country that grows at 3% per year will double every 23 years. After 70 years, the 2% country will have quadrupled its income (2^2 = 4), while the 3% country will have increased its income by a factor of 2^(70/23) ≈ 2^3 = 8 times. The gap is huge. This is why policies that boost growth by even 0.5 percentage points are incredibly valuable. The difference between 1.5% and 2.0% growth over a lifetime (say 50 years) is a 28% higher income. The difference between 2.0% and 2.5% is another 28%. Small changes matter enormously.
principles.application: Policymakers should prioritize policies that boost long-run growth, even if the effects are modest. A 0.5 percentage point increase in growth may seem small, but over a generation it translates into significantly higher living standards. Policies that promote education, infrastructure, R&D, trade openness, and good institutions are worthwhile even if their growth effects are modest. The Bank of Canada cannot affect long-run growth (it focuses on inflation), but the federal government can through structural policies.
principles.example: Canada's per capita growth has averaged about 1.5% since 2000. If Canada could boost growth to 2.0%, after 50 years, income per capita would be 28% higher. That means an additional \$15,000 per person (from \$55,000 to \$70,000). This is a massive improvement in living standards.

image.alt: Graph: GDP per capita over 50 years for growth rates of 1.0%, 1.5%, 2.0%, 2.5%; widening gap shows importance of small differences.
image.caption: Figure 13.1.7: Small differences in growth rates compound into large differences in living standards over time. A 0.5 percentage point difference in growth can mean 28% higher income over 50 years.

principles.principle: Long-Run Growth Is Driven by Supply-Side Factors, Not Demand
principles.statement: In the short run, output is determined by aggregate demand (monetary and fiscal policy). In the long run, output is determined by supply-side factors: capital accumulation, labor force growth, and technological progress. This is the classical dichotomy applied to growth. Monetary policy cannot affect long-run growth; it can only smooth business cycles. Fiscal policy can affect long-run growth if it boosts investment (infrastructure, education, R&D) or improves incentives (tax reform). The Bank of Canada's inflation-targeting framework is designed to stabilize the economy in the short run, leaving long-run growth to be determined by real factors. The key to raising living standards over the long term is to increase productivity: output per worker or output per hour worked. Productivity growth is the ultimate source of rising living standards.
principles.application: Policymakers should distinguish between policies that affect short-run demand (monetary policy, temporary fiscal stimulus) and policies that affect long-run supply (education, infrastructure, R&D, tax reform, trade agreements). The former are for stabilizing the economy; the latter are for raising growth. Canada's low productivity growth (compared to the US) is a concern; policies to boost innovation, competition, and investment are needed.
principles.example: From 2000 to 2024, Canada's labor productivity grew at about 1.0% per year, while US labor productivity grew at about 1.5% per year. This difference explains why US incomes have pulled ahead of Canada. Policies to boost productivity (e.g., R&D tax credits, better education, reduced internal trade barriers) could close this gap.

image.alt: Diagram: Supply-side factors (capital, labor, technology, institutions) → potential output → long-run growth.
image.caption: Figure 13.1.8: Long-run growth is driven by supply-side factors: capital accumulation, labor force growth, technological progress, and institutions. Demand-side policies (monetary, fiscal) affect short-run fluctuations but not long-run growth.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Growth Rates and Doubling Times

image.alt: Table: Real GDP per capita for Canada (2000-2024); growth rate calculations; rule of 70.
image.caption: Figure 13.1.9: Worked example: calculating growth rates and using the rule of 70 to estimate doubling times.

worked_examples.problem:
Canada's real GDP per capita (in 2017 CAD) was:

• 2000: \$45,000
• 2010: \$50,000
• 2020: \$55,000
• 2024: \$57,000

a) Calculate the annual growth rate from 2000 to 2010 (using the average annual growth formula).
b) Calculate the annual growth rate from 2010 to 2020.
c) Calculate the annual growth rate from 2020 to 2024.
d) Using the rule of 70, how many years would it take for Canada's GDP per capita to double at the 2000-2010 growth rate?
e) If Canada's growth rate increased from 1.0% to 2.0% per year, how much higher would GDP per capita be after 40 years?

step_by_step.explanation:
Rule of 70: Doubling time = 70 / growth rate (%) = 70 / 1.06 = 66.0 years

At Canada's 2000-2010 growth rate, it would take about 66 years for GDP per capita to double.

step_by_step.step: Impact of increasing growth from 1.0% to 2.0% over 40 years
step_by_step.explanation:
At 1.0% growth over 40 years: $Y_{40} = Y_0 \times (1.01)^{40} = Y_0 \times 1.489$ (48.9% increase)

At 2.0% growth over 40 years: $Y_{40} = Y_0 \times (1.02)^{40} = Y_0 \times 2.208$ (120.8% increase)

The ratio: 2.208 / 1.489 = 1.483

At 2.0% growth, GDP per capita would be 48.3% higher than at 1.0% growth after 40 years. This illustrates the power of even a 1 percentage point difference in growth rates.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) 1.06%. b) 0.957%. c) 0.896%. d) 66 years. e) 48.3% higher after 40 years.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying growth rate calculations and the rule of 70 to analyze Canada's economic growth and the impact of growth rate differences.

worked_examples.title: Comparing Growth Across Countries

image.alt: Table: Initial GDP per capita ($5,000), growth rates (1%, 2%, 3%, 4%, 5%); GDP after 30 and 60 years.
image.caption: Figure 13.1.10: Worked example: comparing GDP per capita after 30 and 60 years for countries with different growth rates.

worked_examples.problem:
Four countries start with the same GDP per capita of \$5,000 in Year 0. They grow at different annual rates:

• Country A: 1% per year
• Country B: 2% per year
• Country C: 3% per year
• Country D: 4% per year

a) Calculate GDP per capita after 30 years for each country.
b) Calculate GDP per capita after 60 years for each country.
c) How many times richer is Country D than Country A after 60 years?
d) How many years would it take for Country B to catch up to Country A's starting level if Country A had zero growth?
e) If Country A grows at 1% and Country B grows at 3%, how many years until Country B's GDP per capita is twice Country A's?

step_by_step.step: Country D vs. Country A after 60 years
step_by_step.explanation:
Country D: \$52,600, Country A: \$9,085.

Ratio = 52,600 / 9,085 = 5.79 times richer.

A 4% growth rate (D) vs. 1% growth rate (A) leads to nearly 6 times higher income after 60 years.

step_by_step.step: Catch-up time (Country B vs. Country A with zero growth)
step_by_step.explanation:
Country A has zero growth (stays at \$5,000). Country B grows at 2% per year.

We want $5,000 \times (1.02)^t = 5,000$? That would be t=0. Actually we want B to reach A's starting level? That's already true at t=0. If A has zero growth, B surpasses A immediately. The question might be: how long for B to reach A's starting level if A had zero growth? That is trivial (t=0).

Let's rephrase: If Country A had zero growth (stays at \$5,000) and Country B grows at 2%, how long until B's GDP per capita is twice A's? That is $5,000 \times (1.02)^t = 10,000$ → $(1.02)^t = 2$ → $t = \ln(2)/\ln(1.02) = 0.6931/0.0198 = 35.0 years.

step_by_step.step: Time for Country B to be twice Country A (both growing)
step_by_step.explanation:
A: 1% growth, B: 3% growth. We want $5,000 \times (1.03)^t = 2 \times 5,000 \times (1.01)^t$

Cancel 5,000: $(1.03)^t = 2 \times (1.01)^t$

$(1.03/1.01)^t = 2$

$(1.0198)^t = 2$

$t = \ln(2) / \ln(1.0198) = 0.6931 / 0.0196 = 35.4 years$

After about 35 years, Country B's GDP per capita will be twice Country A's, even though both are growing.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying compound growth calculations to compare living standards across countries with different growth rates.

process_flow.description: Obtain real GDP and population figures from national statistical agencies (Statistics Canada, BEA) or international organizations (IMF, World Bank).
process_flow.mechanism: Real GDP from national accounts; population from census or estimates.

image.alt: Diagram: Data sources (Statistics Canada, BEA, IMF) → Y and N.
image.caption: Figure 13.1.11: Step 1 - Collect real GDP and population data from statistical agencies or international organizations.

process_flow.description: Divide real GDP by population to get output per person.

image.alt: Equation: GDP per capita = Y / N.
image.caption: Figure 13.1.12: Step 2 - Calculate real GDP per capita by dividing real GDP by population.

process_flow.description: Compute the annual percentage change in real GDP per capita between years.

image.caption: Figure 13.1.13: Step 3 - Calculate the annual growth rate of real GDP per capita.

process_flow.process: Estimate doubling time using rule of 70
process_flow.description: Use the rule of 70 to estimate how many years it would take for GDP per capita to double at the current growth rate.
process_flow.mechanism: Doubling time = 70 / g (where g is in percent).

image.caption: Figure 13.1.14: Step 4 - Use the rule of 70 to estimate the doubling time at the current growth rate.

process_flow.process: Compare across countries or time periods
process_flow.description: Compare growth rates and living standards across countries to identify growth miracles and growth disasters.
process_flow.mechanism: Cross-country comparison of GDP per capita and growth rates.

image.alt: Diagram: Compare growth rates and GDP per capita across countries.
image.caption: Figure 13.1.15: Step 5 - Compare growth rates and living standards across countries or time periods.

image.alt: Master framework: Collect data → calculate GDP per capita → compute growth rate → estimate doubling time → compare across countries.
image.caption: Figure 13.1.16: Complete framework for defining and measuring economic growth.

summary: Economic growth is the increase in the quantity and quality of goods and services produced by an economy over time, typically measured as the annual percentage change in real GDP per capita. Real GDP is adjusted for inflation; real GDP per capita accounts for population changes and is the best measure of average living standards. The rule of 70 states that doubling time = 70 / growth rate (%), illustrating the power of compounding: even small differences in growth rates lead to large differences in living standards over time. Long-run growth is about increases in potential output (driven by capital accumulation, labor force growth, and technological progress), while short-run fluctuations (business cycles) are about actual output deviating from potential. Monetary policy can smooth business cycles but cannot affect long-run growth; fiscal policy can affect both (through infrastructure, education, R&D). Understanding economic growth is essential for analyzing why some countries are rich and others poor, for evaluating policies that affect long-run prosperity, and for appreciating the importance of even modest increases in growth rates.
